title: The Value of AI-Generated Metadata for UGC Platforms: Evidence from a Large-scale Field Experiment

study_design: To answer these questions, we collaborated with a leading short-video platform in Asia (hereafter "Platform A") to conduct a large-scale randomized field experiment.
In our experiment, users were randomly assigned to either the treatment or the control condition.
This experiment lasted from July 20 th to August 21 st, 2023.

Content producers in the treatment group received access to AI-generated titles on the video posting page, and could adjust the titles as needed, whereas producers in the control group could only write titles by themselves.

To verify the randomization effectiveness, we compared treatment producers ( N =1,024,940) and control producers ( N =1,023,093) on their pre-treatment video engagement outcomes, producer characteristics, and video attributes.
The results of pairwise t-tests in Table 3 show no significant differences between treatment and control groups on these observable attributes.
task: Users on Platform A primarily consume videos recommended by the platform.
Content producers post short videos on Platform A to enhance viewership/or engagement and attract new followers, aiming to increase advertising opportunities and revenue.
Users visit Platform A either to be entertained by videos that catch their interest (organic browse) or to search for specific videos related to a topic (search-oriented browse).
When posting videos, producers are encouraged to add titles with hashtags or descriptions in the title-setting box (see Figure 3(a)).

Treatment group producers could access an AI-generated title in the title-setting box on the video posting page after they uploaded the video (see Figure 3(b)), and there is a notification next to the AI-generated title indicating that the provided title is generated by AI.

Treatment producers had the flexibility to either delete, amend, or fully adopt the AI-generated title, and regardless of their choices, viewers could not see any indication in their interface of whether the titles were generated by AI.
population: Our study included 2 , 048, 033 producers who posted at least one video during our treatment period, with 1,024,940 in the treatment group and 1,023,093 in the control group.
During the treatment period, producers in the treatment group uploaded 5,377,560 videos, while those in the control group posted 5,361,424 videos.
We collaborated with one of the largest short-video platforms in Asia (Platform A), which has over 300 million daily active users.
We surveyed 1,925 treatment group users with open-ended questions on the usage of AI-generated titles.
arms: Content producers in the treatment group received access to AI-generated titles on the video posting page, and could adjust the titles as needed, whereas producers in the control group could only write titles by themselves.

Treatment group producers could access an AI-generated title in the title-setting box on the video posting page after they uploaded the video (see Figure 3(b)), and there is a notification next to the AI-generated title indicating that the provided title is generated by AI.
In contrast, control group producers could not access such a tool to generate titles via AI (see Figure 3(a)).
Our study included 2 , 048, 033 producers who posted at least one video during our treatment period, with 1,024,940 in the treatment group and 1,023,093 in the control group.
outcome_definitions: Dependent variables, which capture viewers' video consumption, include viewership metrics such as the number of valid watches (ValidWatchij), and viewers' total watch duration in minutes ( WatchDurationij).

For videos between 3 and less than 7 seconds, a viewer watch is valid if the watch duration matches the video duration. For videos of 7 seconds or longer, this count is recorded if the watch duration is at least 7 seconds. This metric is designed by the platform to measure the consumption of a video.

To accommodate variations in video posting times during the treatment period, we calculated the cumulative viewership outcomes for each video over the first two weeks after its posting (Zeng et al. 2023).

To evaluate user-video matching accuracy, we used the Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC), a widely applied metric in recommender system studies (Chen et al. 2024, Bi et al. 2024). AUC measures how well the model predicts viewer engagement behaviors by comparing the predicted and actual viewer engagement outcomes.

We followed prior research (Qiao et al. 2020) and measured it through multiple dimensions, including lexical density ( LexicalDensityij), lexical variation ( LexicalV ariationij), and entropy (Entropy ij).

Lexical density is the proportion of content words (such as nouns, verbs, and adjectives) to the total number of words in a text. Lexical variation is the ratio of unique words to the total number of words.
statistical_results: Our findings show that the provision of AI-generated titles significantly boosted content consumption, increasing valid watches by 1.6% and watch duration by 0.9%.
When producers adopted these titles, these increases jumped to 7.1% and 4.1% respectively.
This viewership-boost effect was largely attributed to the use of this generative AI (GAI) tool increasing the likelihood of videos having a title by 41.4%.
The effect was more pronounced for the groups more affected by metadata sparsity.
Specifically, access to AI-generated titles increased the likelihood of a video having a title by 41.4% and enhances the probability of having tags by 72.4%.
Column (1) indicates an increase of 1.6% in valid watches in the treatment group compared to the control group ( β1 = 0.016, p-value < 0.01).
Results in column (2) indicate that treatment group videos enhanced watch duration by 0.9% from the control group (β1 = 0.009, p-value < 0.01).
The positive coefficients ofAdoptij indicate that adopting AI-generated titles increased valid watches by 7.1% ( p-value<0.01), and watch duration by 4.1% (p-value<0.01).
Utilitarian-content videos in the treatment group experienced a relative decrease in valid watches by 3.1% (p-value<0.01), and watch duration by 3.0% (p-value<0.01) than hedonic-content videos.

Overall, utilitarian-content videos in the treatment group showed a 1.4% decrease in valid watches, and a 1.9% decrease in watch duration, which is likely due to the more detailed human-generated metadata already associated with utilitarian-content videos.

Low-skilled producers, compared to high-skilled ones, experienced an increase of 1.61% in valid watches ( p-value<0.01), and 1.31% in watch duration ( p-value<0.01) due to the access to AI-generated titles.
Utilitarian-content videos showed a relative decrease of 14.0% in valid watches (p-value<0.01), and 13.2% in watch duration (p-value<0.01) compared with hedonic-content videos.

In contrast, low-skilled producers, relative to high-skilled ones, received an incremental increase of 6.7% in valid watches ( p-value<0.01), and 5.5% in watch duration ( p-value<0.01) due to the adoption of AI-generated titles.
However, the net effect was negative for utilitarian-content videos, with a decrease of 1.2% in valid watches, and 8.8% in watch duration.
The AUC for shares increased from 0.823 in the control group to 0.848 in the treatment group, an improvement of 0.026 ( p-value<0.01).
For likes, the AUC rose from 0.892 to 0.921, an increase of 0.029 (p-value<0.01), and for follows, it increased from 0.867 to 0.887, a rise of 0.019 ( p-value<0.01).
Interestingly, the results in Table 12 show that videos in the treatment group experienced a decrease of 37.9% in valid watches and 32.6% in watch duration compared to the control group.

The negative coefficient of the interaction term in Table 13 indicates that a 10% increase in similarity between AI- and human-generated titles led to an 9.8% decrease in valid watches, and a 8.2% decrease in watch duration.

However, interestingly, when considering the positive coefficient for T reati, we find that treatment group videos with less than 20.8% and 20.9% similarity (i.e., low overlap with AI-generated titles) outperformed the control group in both valid watches and watch duration.
Each 10% decrease in textual similarity between AI-generated titles and actual titles adopted by the producers increased the video valid watch by 9.8% in valid watches and watch duration by 9.2%.

Moreover, lower similarity scores were also associated with richer linguistic attributes, where a 10% decrease in similarity correlated with a 4.8% increase in lexical density, 3.7% in lexical variation, and 1.1% in entropy.
The results in Table 14 show that videos in the treatment group outperformed those in the control group in terms of valid watches and watch duration.
The results in Table 15 show that each 10% increase in similarity score related to a 4.8%, 3.7%, and 1.1% decrease in lexical density, lexical variation, and entropy, respectively.
The treatment group with access to AI-generated titles had a significantly lower HHI of 0.0002 compared to 0.0003 in the control group, representing a 50% reduction in platform-level HHI.

The results, presented in Table 20 and 21, show a 0.3% increase in both valid watches and watch duration for treatment group videos in the search-oriented channel, and a stronger 1.5% increase in valid watches with a 0.9% increase in watch duration in the organic channel.
As shown in Table 22, there is no statistically significant difference in the number of videos produced (p-value > 0.1) and the time gaps between videos (p-value > 0.1) across the two groups.
This first-stage F -statistic of 1,700,000.
ambiguity_or_caveats: Due to some technical issues, Platform A only stored the AI-generated titles between August 8th and August 21 st during the experiment.

While our main analysis focuses on viewership outcomes (e.g., valid watch and watch duration), this additional dataset lacks predicted probabilities for these measures. Instead, it includes predictions for downstream engagement behaviors such as liking, sharing, and following.
As these behaviors occur at later stages of the user journey, their accurate predictions imply that viewership outcomes, which occur earlier, are also likely to be predicted accurately.

To protect Platform A's sensitive information, the mean values of four key variables (ValidWatch, WatchDuration, Follower, and Following) presented in the tables have been scaled by multiplying some positive numbers.

There is an endogenity concern that the videos with significantly revised AI-generated titles, resulting in lower similarity between the AI-generated titles and actual titles adopted by producer, may inherently indicate higher producer effort and video quality.
We removed 2,226,922 titled videos (51.56% of the total titled videos) in our treatment group that did not receive AI-generated titles due to algorithmic issues.

In 2023, external GAI tools for generating video titles were unlikely to be widely used since major language models did not support video processing, and few video platforms offered such features. Thus, the risk of contamination, where the control group was unintentionally influenced by the experimental intervention, was limited.
Rather than directly processing raw video streams, which is computationally intensive, Platform A uses extracted features from these streams in its recommender system.

First, while we focus on the value of AI-generated metadata in improving user-content matching, we only explore content-based metadata. Future research could explore the role of user-related metadata, such as AI-generated user profiles.
Second, in our study, the GAI algorithm generated titles solely based on video content, without incorporating producer attributes or their historical content.

notes: This is a large-scale randomized field experiment conducted on a leading short-video platform in Asia. The experiment ran from July 20 to August 21, 2023, with 2,048,033 producers (1,024,940 treatment, 1,023,093 control). The study examines the causal effects of AI-generated video titles on content consumption through both intention-to-treat (ITT) and local average treatment effect (LATE) analyses. Key findings include: (1) access to AI-generated titles increased valid watches by 1.6% and watch duration by 0.9% (ITT); (2) adoption of AI-generated titles increased valid watches by 7.1% and watch duration by 4.1% (LATE); (3) the mechanism operates through improved user-video matching accuracy as measured by AUC; (4) AI-generated titles were lower quality than human-generated ones for videos that already had titles (37.9% decrease in valid watches); (5) human-AI co-creation with significant revisions to AI-generated titles outperformed both fully AI-generated and human-only approaches. The study also includes mechanism analysis using AUC metrics, heterogeneous effects analysis by content type and producer skill level, lexical richness analysis, viewership diversity analysis using HHI, channel analysis (organic vs. search-oriented recommendations), and multiple robustness checks with alternative variable operationalizations.
